text.title: What You Don't Know About Launch Pre-Mortem Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every What You Don't Know About Launch Pre-Mortem application MUST enforce, plus a terminal skip-this-methodology rule.

rule.statement: Pre-mortem enumerates ≥6 launch surfaces (billing, auth, data, support, abuse, retention). Skipping a surface requires a named reason.
rule.rationale: Unknown-unknowns hide in the surface that was never named.

rule.statement: Every identified failure mode names a single accountable fix owner (handle + role). Placeholder values or 'team' are rejected.
rule.rationale: Pre-mortem with anonymous owners produces no fixes; the failure is just re-documented post-mortem.

rule.statement: Every failure mode names a concrete detector signal (log, alert, metric, ticket pattern) the team can monitor.
rule.rationale: Failures with no detector are unrecoverable; the detector pins which failures we can survive.

rule.statement: The pre-mortem report carries a launch-kill criterion: a single sentence that, if true, blocks GO. No-kill-criterion launches turn pre-mortems into theatre.
rule.rationale: Kill criteria force the team to define what 'not safe' means before pressing GO.

rule.statement: Pre-mortem is refreshed within 24h of launch-week start. Pre-mortems older than 14 days are flagged stale.
rule.rationale: Launch context drifts fast; an old pre-mortem misses the surfaces that changed last week.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition or triggered skip, then route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
